_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Cardiovascular: Tachycardia with a Pulse. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Tachycardia with a Pulse

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Tachycardia with a Pulse
points: Maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion
Control ventricular rate
Restore regular sinus rhythm in unstable patient
Search for underlying cause: a. Medications (caffeine, diet pills, thyroid, decongestants)

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Heart rate greater than 100 BPM in adults or relative tachycardia in pediatric patients
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Sinus tachycardia

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98%
CPR and AED if patient becomes pulseless (cardioversion not available at EMR)
Position of comfort; supine if hypotensive

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: 12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
Vagal maneuvers for narrow complex SVT (Valsalva, modified Valsalva)
IV maintenance (non-medicated)
CPAP for tachycardia with pulmonary edema

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV fluid bolus for tachycardia from hypovolemia
Waveform capnography
Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push for narrow complex regular SVT (per state/local protocol at AEMT level)

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push for narrow complex regular SVT; 12 mg if no response after 1-2 min (use proximal site, rapid flush)
Synchronized cardioversion for unstable tachycardia (VT with pulse, SVT with hemodynamic compromise)
Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min for stable wide complex tachycardia
Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO for pulseless VT/VF
Procainamide 20-50 mg/min IV (max 17 mg/kg) for stable wide complex tachycardia
12-lead ECG interpretation: differentiate SVT, AFib, WPW, VT
A-fib/flutter with rapid ventricular response: rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol) per protocol
RSI and intubation for airway compromise in unstable tachyarrhythmia

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Only use one antidysrhythmic at a time
Patients who receive beta-blockers (e.g., metoprolol) with calcium channel blockers (e.g., diltiazem) are at increased risk for hypotension and bradycardia
If using cardioversion, consider sedation and pain control

With irregular wide complex tachycardia (A-FIB with aberrancy such as Wolff-Parkinson-White and Lown-Ganong Levine), avoid use of AV nodal blocking agents (e.g., adenosine, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers)
Patients with Wolff'Parkinson'White should be given procainamide prior to amiodarone

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Causes: a. Hypovolemia
A-FIB rarely requires cardioversion in the field. As it is difficult to ascertain the onset of this rhythm, the risk of stroke needs to be considered prior to cardioversion

A wide-complex irregular rhythm should be considered pre-excited A-FIB; extreme care must be taken in these patients a. Characteristic EKG findings include a short PR interval and, in some cases, a delta wave

Amiodarone or procainamide can be used as a rate-controlling agent for patients who are intolerant of or unresponsive to other agents, such as patients with CHF who may not otherwise tolerate diltiazem or metoprolol a. Caution should be exercised in those who are not receiving anticoagulation, as amiodarone can promote cardioversion

Administer metoprolol to patients with SBP greater than 120 mmHg a. Worsening CHF, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), asthma, as well as hypotension and bradycardia can occur with use of metoprolol

Biphasic waveforms have been proven to convert A-FIB at lower energies and higher rates of success than monophasic waveforms a. Strategies include dose escalation (70, 120, 150, 170 joules (J) for biphasic or 100, 200, 300, 360 J for monophasic) versus beginning with single high energy/highest success rate for single shock delivered
Studies in infants and children have demonstrated the effectiveness of adenosine for the treatment of hemodynamically stable or unstable SVT
Adenosine should be considered the preferred medication for stable SVT a. Verapamil may be considered as alternative therapy in older children but should not be routinely used in infants

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes
' Time, dose, and response to medications given
' Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response
' Obtain monitor strips after each intervention
' Patient weight
' Pediatric length-based tape color (for pediatrics who fit on tape)
' History of event supporting treatment of underlying causes

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
